content.problemStatement:
In Uttu's college, a semester is said to be a:


**Overload** semester if the number of credits taken >65\gt 65>65.
**Underload** semester if the number of credits taken <35\lt 35<35.
**Normal** semester otherwise
Given the number of credits XXX taken by Uttu, determine whether the semester is `Overload`, `Underload` or `Normal`.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first and only of each test case contains a single integer XXX - the number of credits taken by Uttu.

You may print each character of `Overload`, `Underload` and `Normal` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `ovErLoAd`, `oVERlOAD`, `OVERLOAD` will be considered identical).



Output Format
For each test case, output `Overload`, `Underload` or `Normal` depending upon the number of credits taken by Uttu.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer X, the number of credits taken by Uttu.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one of the words: Overload, Underload, or Normal, according to the classification of X.

sampleTestCases.explanation: 65 is between 35 and 65 inclusive → Normal. 80 > 65 → Overload. 23 < 35 → Underload. 58 is within the normal range → Normal.
34 < 35 → Underload. 66 > 65 → Overload. 35 is exactly the lower bound → Normal.
1 is far below 35 → Underload. 100 exceeds 65 → Overload.

solution.approach: Read the number of test cases. For each test case read X. If X > 65 output "Overload". Else if X < 35 output "Underload". Otherwise output "Normal". The algorithm is O(T) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – each test case is processed in constant time.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
